items.title: United Kingdom
items.byline: Westminster model: parliamentary sovereignty, devolution, and rule of law since 1688.
items.key_takeaway: The UK is the course's anchor liberal democracy: parliamentary sovereignty, rule of law, and devolution make it the benchmark for comparing all other regime types.
items.summary: The United Kingdom is a unitary state with a parliamentary system in which executive power is fused with the legislature — the Prime Minister and Cabinet must maintain the confidence of the elected House of Commons. Despite formal parliamentary sovereignty, a robust independent Supreme Court, common law tradition, and free press distinguish the UK as the course's clearest example of rule of law. Devolution has transferred significant powers to the Scottish Parliament, Welsh Senedd, and Northern Ireland Assembly without formally dismantling the unitary structure. The 2016 Brexit referendum and the 2014 Scottish independence referendum illustrate how referenda function as direct-democracy tools in an otherwise representative system. Compared to other course countries, the UK has the most private control of natural resources and a positive net migration rate that has generated domestic political pressure. It serves as the primary comparative anchor for parliamentary systems, rule of law, civil society strength, and post-materialist values.
required_cases: Devolution to Scotland, Wales, and Northern Ireland
2016 Brexit referendum and EU departure (2020)
2014 Scottish independence referendum
Vote of no confidence mechanism
House of Lords as appointed upper chamber
Common law judicial system
NHS and aging population demographic challenge
Positive net migration and social tensions
comparative_themes: FPTP electoral system and two-party tendency

paired_with.title: United Mexican States
paired_with.relation: Democratic comparison: both transitioned from concentrated power to competitive multiparty systems via institutional reform

paired_with.title: Federal Republic of Nigeria
paired_with.relation: Federal vs. unitary contrast; both Commonwealth-linked; shared common-law judicial heritage

paired_with.title: Russian Federation
paired_with.relation: Parliamentary vs. semi-presidential comparison; democratic vs. hybrid regime contrast

items.exam_appearance: Frequently appears as the primary example of a parliamentary system, rule of law, devolution, and referenda. Commonly paired with Russia in system-type FRQs and with Mexico for electoral-system comparisons. Anchor country for civil society, post-materialism, and FPTP disproportionality questions.

sections.label: Government Structure
sections.title: Parliamentary Sovereignty and Devolution
sections.blurb: The UK operates under a parliamentary system in which the Prime Minister and Cabinet are drawn from and accountable to the elected House of Commons. The monarch serves as ceremonial head of state and formally appoints the PM but has no meaningful governing power. Parliament is formally supreme — there is no written constitution above it — yet the independent Supreme Court enforces rule of law, resolves devolution disputes, and protects human rights. Devolution has transferred legislative authority to the Scottish Parliament, Welsh Senedd, and Northern Ireland Assembly, but Westminster retains the formal right to override those devolved bodies, confirming the UK's status as a unitary rather than federal state. This combination of parliamentary fusion of powers, uncodified constitutional norms, and administrative devolution makes the UK structurally distinctive among course countries.
sections.adi_prompt: How does parliamentary sovereignty differ from a written constitution, and what keeps the UK government accountable without one?

sections.label: Electoral System
sections.title: FPTP, Referenda, and the Lords
sections.blurb: Members of the House of Commons are elected by first-past-the-post (FPTP) in 650 single-member districts. This system tends to produce a two-party dominant legislature by over-rewarding the top vote-getter and wasting votes for smaller parties nationally — though regional parties like the SNP can win seats by concentrating votes geographically. The unelected House of Lords is appointed through a process involving the monarch, Prime Minister, and an independent commission; it can delay but not permanently block Commons legislation. The UK has also used binding referenda for major constitutional questions: devolution to Scotland (1997), Wales (1997), Northern Ireland (1998), the Scottish independence question (2014), and Brexit (2016). These referenda exemplify how direct-democracy tools operate within a representative parliamentary system.
sections.adi_prompt: Why does first-past-the-post voting tend to produce a two-party system in the UK, and why are smaller parties disadvantaged?

sections.label: Party System
sections.title: Two-Party Dominance and Strong Party Discipline
sections.blurb: The Conservative and Labour parties dominate the House of Commons and alternate control of government. Minor parties — including the Liberal Democrats, Scottish National Party, Plaid Cymru, and the Democratic Unionist Party — can win seats through geographic concentration, but FPTP structurally limits their national seat share relative to their vote share. Party discipline is exceptionally strong in the UK: MPs are expected to vote with their party whip, and defection risks loss of the party endorsement needed to retain one's seat. This combination of two-party dominance, strong discipline, and parliamentary fusion of powers means that a governing majority can legislate efficiently with relatively few institutional vetoes, contrasting sharply with Mexico's more fragmented multiparty system or Russia's managed-democracy party landscape.
sections.adi_prompt: What is party discipline, and why is it stronger in the UK's parliamentary system than in presidential systems?

sections.label: Civil Society
sections.title: Strong and Autonomous Civil Society
sections.blurb: The UK has one of the course's most robust civil societies — a broad network of independent NGOs, trade unions, media organizations, religious bodies, and professional associations that operate freely from state control. This autonomous civil society serves as an agent of democratization and a check on state power, exposing governmental malfeasance and channeling citizen demands. Press freedom is high by comparative standards, and the government does not control broadcast or print media. Civil society includes post-materialist movements focused on environmental protection and social equality, consistent with the UK's high development level. Scottish nationalism, Northern Irish communal tensions, and racial cleavages from Britain's colonial history represent social divisions that civil society organizations also navigate. The UK contrasts sharply with China and Russia, where civil society is monitored, registered, and restricted.
sections.adi_prompt: How does a strong civil society help maintain democracy, and what would happen if the UK suddenly restricted NGOs the way Russia does?

sections.label: Political Economy
sections.title: Most Private Resource Control, Brexit, and NHS Pressures
sections.blurb: Among the six course countries, the UK allows the most private control of natural resources — energy, utilities, and industry are largely market-driven rather than state-owned. Brexit, formalized in 2020 after the 2016 referendum, represents both a cultural backlash against globalization and a reassertion of national sovereignty over supranational rules, particularly EU single-market regulations. The UK operates an NHS (National Health Service), and an aging population combined with declining working-age ratios has increased pressure on its universal healthcare funding model, requiring difficult policy trade-offs. Positive net immigration has been economically beneficial but has generated domestic political tensions and empowered nationalist parties, illustrating how demographic change interacts with political legitimacy. The UK's departure from the EU also demonstrates how membership in a supranational organization can constrain domestic policy choices.
sections.adi_prompt: How did Brexit illustrate the tension between national sovereignty and membership in a supranational organization like the EU?

adi_prompts.quiz: Quiz me on the United Kingdom: ask me about its system of government, how the Prime Minister is selected, what devolution means, and how the UK compares to other course countries on rule of law.
adi_prompts.explain: Explain how the UK's parliamentary system, rule of law, and devolution make it the course's primary example of a liberal democracy — and how it compares to Russia's semi-presidential system.

items.title: Russian Federation
items.byline: Managed democracy: elections held but systematically manipulated to ensure Putin's United Russia dominates.
items.key_takeaway: Russia is the course's defining hybrid regime: elections exist on paper but are undermined by registration restrictions, media control, threshold rules, and judicial manipulation that guarantee United Russia's dominance.
items.summary: Russia operates as a semi-presidential system with a popularly elected president who holds far more real power than the prime minister. While formal democratic institutions — a bicameral legislature, popular elections, and a constitution — exist, the regime is classified as a competitive authoritarian or managed democracy because elections are systematically manipulated. The six mechanisms of managed democracy (party registration requirements, media nationalization, threshold rules, selective court decisions, elimination of gubernatorial elections, and federal district envoys) ensure United Russia's dominance. Putin's consolidation of power included re-nationalizing oil and gas industries after their post-Soviet privatization, making the state a rentier actor dependent on hydrocarbon revenues. Russia's judiciary, though constitutionally granted review powers, is used to target political opposition rather than constrain government. Civil society faces foreign-agent laws and registration requirements that severely restrict its autonomy. Russia's post-Soviet political culture — marked by tolerance for strong leadership and low institutional trust — shapes citizen behavior in ways that distinguish it from both the UK's liberal democracy and China's one-party state.
required_cases: United Russia party dominance and managed democracy mechanisms
Semi-presidential system: dual executive (president + prime minister)
State Duma elections: mixed SMD + PR system with threshold
Federation Council: appointed, not directly elected
2011–12 election fraud protests undermining legitimacy
Putin's re-nationalization of oil and gas industries
State media nationalization and control of broadcast outlets
Foreign-agent laws restricting civil society
2020 constitutional amendments extending presidential term limits
Chechen separatist conflicts as internal actors threatening stability
comparative_themes: state media and restricted civil liberties

paired_with.title: People's Republic of China
paired_with.relation: Authoritarian comparison: both suppress opposition, control media, and use state resources for political dominance — but through different mechanisms

paired_with.title: United Mexican States
paired_with.relation: Democratic transition contrast: both had dominant-party systems, but Mexico genuinely democratized while Russia reversed course

paired_with.title: Islamic Republic of Iran
paired_with.relation: Rentier state and resource curse comparison; both oil-dependent authoritarian/hybrid regimes with external sanctions pressure

items.exam_appearance: Central to FRQ comparisons on semi-presidential systems, managed democracy, and democratic reversal. Frequently paired with Mexico (one-party past, divergent outcomes), China (authoritarian tools), and the UK (executive system type). Appears in MCQs on civil society restrictions, media nationalization, rentier state, and electoral manipulation mechanisms.

sections.label: Government Structure
sections.title: Semi-Presidential Dual Executive
sections.blurb: Russia's semi-presidential system features two separately elected executives: a popularly elected president (head of state and commander in chief) and a prime minister (head of government) who oversees the civil service and must be confirmed by the State Duma. In practice, the president dominates — appointing top ministers, conducting foreign policy, and controlling security services — while the prime minister's role is subordinate. The State Duma (lower house) passes legislation and confirms the prime minister; the Federation Council (upper house) is appointed by regional governors and regional legislatures rather than directly elected, approving budgets, treaties, and troop deployments. Constitutional amendments in 2020 extended term limits in ways that allow the current president to remain in power through 2036, illustrating how formal constitutional mechanisms can be used to consolidate rather than limit executive authority.
sections.adi_prompt: How is Russia's semi-presidential system different from a purely parliamentary system like the UK's, and why does the president hold more real power than the prime minister?

sections.label: Electoral System
sections.title: Mixed Elections Under Managed Democracy
sections.blurb: Russia's State Duma is elected through a mixed system: half from single-member districts (SMD) by plurality and half by proportional representation with a threshold, limiting small-party access. Presidential elections require an absolute majority, using a runoff if no candidate clears 50 percent in the first round. However, the regime employs at least six managed democracy mechanisms to ensure United Russia's dominance: increasing party registration requirements, allowing only registered parties to compete, using selective court decisions to disqualify opposition candidates, limiting opposition media access, raising electoral thresholds, and appointing presidential envoys to nine federal districts to reassert central control over regions. The 2024 presidential election, in which Putin won approximately 87 percent of the vote, exemplifies how elections in a competitive authoritarian system serve legitimating rather than genuinely competitive functions.
sections.adi_prompt: What are the specific mechanisms Russia uses to manage electoral outcomes, and how do they differ from simply banning elections altogether?

sections.label: Party System
sections.title: United Russia and Dominant-Party Authoritarianism
sections.blurb: United Russia is the dominant party, closely identified with Putin and sustained by state resources, media access, and legal tools rather than genuine popular competition. Multiple other parties legally exist and hold Duma seats, but changing threshold rules, registration barriers, and media exclusion systematically undermine their viability. This stands in contrast to China's formal one-party prohibition — Russia maintains the outward form of multiparty competition while engineering its outcome. The comparison with Mexico is particularly important: both countries had dominant-party systems rooted in patronage and state resources, but Mexico dismantled corporatist structures and created an independent electoral commission (INE) while Russia eliminated gubernatorial elections and expanded presidential appointment powers. Russia represents what Mexico's democratic reversal would have looked like had institutional reforms failed.
sections.adi_prompt: How does United Russia's dominance differ from China's one-party system, and what would it take for Russia to have genuine multiparty competition?

sections.label: Civil Society
sections.title: Restricted Civil Society and State Media
sections.blurb: Civil society in Russia is severely restricted through foreign-agent laws that require NGOs receiving foreign funding to register as foreign agents, subjecting them to audits, labeling requirements, and potential bans. Most broadcast media outlets were nationalized under Putin, giving the state tight control over political messaging while allowing a limited number of opposition news outlets to operate before eventually being suppressed. The 2011–12 protests following disputed State Duma elections demonstrated that civil society and oppositional participation can emerge even in competitive authoritarian states, but the regime responded with new restrictions on protest and expanded registration requirements. Russia's post-Soviet political culture — marked by historically low trust in institutions and tolerance for strong-man leadership — shapes both citizen acceptance of these restrictions and the difficulty of mobilizing sustained democratic opposition.
sections.adi_prompt: Why does Russia restrict civil society organizations through foreign-agent laws rather than simply banning them outright?

sections.label: Political Economy
sections.title: Rentier State, Re-Nationalization, and Sanctions
sections.blurb: Russia is a rentier state — it derives a large proportion of government revenue from oil and gas exports rather than broad-based taxation, reducing accountability to citizens. Following post-Soviet privatization in the 1990s, Putin re-nationalized the oil and gas sector, using state ownership of energy resources as a source of political power and legitimacy. This resource dependence creates revenue volatility tied to world oil prices, lack of economic diversification, and corruption. External economic sanctions — imposed by other governments in response to actions including the annexation of Crimea (2014) — represent a challenge to regime sovereignty from international actors. Cultural backlash against Western economic and cultural influence has fueled nationalist and populist political currents that the regime actively cultivates. Russia exemplifies how resource wealth can substitute for democratic accountability rather than fund it.
sections.adi_prompt: How does being a rentier state reduce government accountability, and why did Putin re-nationalize Russia's oil and gas industry?

adi_prompts.quiz: Quiz me on Russia: ask me about its semi-presidential system, the mechanisms of managed democracy, how United Russia maintains dominance, and how Russia compares to Mexico as a democratic transition case.
adi_prompts.explain: Explain how Russia's electoral system functions as a managed democracy — what specific tools does the regime use to guarantee outcomes while maintaining the appearance of competitive elections?

items.title: People's Republic of China
items.byline: One-party CPC state: rule by law, Great Firewall, and SEZ-driven growth without political liberalization.
items.key_takeaway: China is the course's defining one-party authoritarian state: the Communist Party controls the military, government, and judiciary through rule by law rather than rule of law, while pursuing selective economic liberalization via special economic zones.
items.summary: The People's Republic of China is governed by a one-party authoritarian system in which the Chinese Communist Party (CPC) holds monopoly control over the government, military, and judiciary. Formal state institutions — including the unicameral National People's Congress (NPC), the president, and the premier — are constitutionally described as powerful but in practice defer to the Politburo Standing Committee, the real apex of power. The principle of rule by law means that courts and legislation serve as instruments of party control rather than as independent constraints on state power. China uses the Great Firewall to censor internet content, prevent political organizing, and maintain the party's information monopoly. Economically, China has pursued selective liberalization through special economic zones (SEZs) along its coast, allowing market mechanisms in designated areas while the state retains control elsewhere — making China the course country with the least private control of natural resources overall. Internal cleavages between the Han majority and ethnic minorities (Uighurs in Xinjiang, Tibetans) generate separatist pressures that the regime manages through state repression. China's rapid economic growth has been used as a primary source of regime legitimacy, illustrating that authoritarian regimes can claim popular support through economic performance rather than democratic elections.
required_cases: Politburo Standing Committee as real center of power vs. NPC as rubber-stamp legislature
Rule by law: judiciary subservient to CPC decisions
Great Firewall: internet censorship and political control
Special economic zones (SEZs) along the coast
Indirect elections for NPC membership through layered local processes
Eight minor parties existing under CPC supervision
One-child policy as state demographic intervention
State-directed socialization through education and media
Han/Uighur/Tibetan ethnic cleavages and state repression
Least private resource control among course countries

paired_with.title: Russian Federation
paired_with.relation: Authoritarian comparison: both authoritarian/hybrid states with state-controlled media and suppressed opposition, but China uses legal prohibition while Russia uses manufactured dominance

paired_with.title: Islamic Republic of Iran
paired_with.relation: One-party vs. theocratic authoritarian comparison; both have unicameral legislatures with constrained independence; both least-private resource control

paired_with.title: United Mexican States
paired_with.relation: Federal vs. unitary contrast; economic liberalization comparison (SEZs vs. NAFTA/maquiladoras); both experienced dominant-party rule, but Mexico transitioned

items.exam_appearance: Appears in nearly every unit as the primary one-party authoritarian example. Used in MCQs on NPC vs. Politburo Standing Committee (real vs. formal power), rule by law vs. rule of law, Great Firewall as media restriction, SEZs as selective economic liberalization, and ethnic cleavages. Frequently paired with Russia for authoritarian comparison FRQs.

sections.label: Government Structure
sections.title: CPC Control Through Dual Party–State Structure
sections.blurb: China's governing structure fuses party and state: the Chinese Communist Party controls all branches of government through a parallel hierarchy. The president simultaneously serves as General Secretary of the CPC and chair of the Central Military Commission, consolidating party, state, and military authority in one position. The NPC is constitutionally designated the most powerful institution, but it meets once per year, ratifies decisions already made by the Politburo Standing Committee, and has never rejected a major CPC policy. The Politburo Standing Committee — composed of seven senior party leaders — is the actual center of power, making governing decisions between NPC sessions. The Standing Committee of the NPC assumes legislative duties most of the year and interprets the constitution. This formal-vs-real power gap is one of the most frequently tested concepts on the AP Comp Gov exam.
sections.adi_prompt: Why does China's constitution call the NPC the most powerful institution if the Politburo Standing Committee is actually in charge?

sections.label: Electoral System
sections.title: Indirect Elections and One-Party Ballot Control
sections.blurb: China uses a system of indirect elections: citizens vote for local delegates, who elect delegates at higher levels, who eventually select NPC members — insulating top leadership from direct popular accountability. NPC elections are not competitive in a democratic sense; only CPC-approved candidates and members of the eight recognized minor parties appear on ballots. The eight minor parties exist legally and hold minor offices under CPC supervision but cannot challenge CPC governance. China does not hold competitive presidential elections; leadership transitions occur through internal CPC processes rather than popular votes. This structure contrasts sharply with Iran's candidate-vetting system — China formally prohibits competitive parties entirely, while Iran holds direct elections that are filtered through the Guardian Council. Both achieve the same outcome of non-competitive selection by different institutional designs.
sections.adi_prompt: How do China's indirect elections differ from Iran's direct elections filtered by the Guardian Council — and what do they have in common in terms of outcomes?

sections.label: Party System
sections.title: One-Party System with Eight Consultative Minor Parties
sections.blurb: China has a formal one-party system: only the CPC can hold governing power, as codified in the constitution. Eight minor parties legally exist and participate in consultative bodies like the Chinese People's Political Consultative Conference, but they cannot run independent candidates for national leadership or challenge CPC policies. Their function is to broaden the appearance of consultation and to represent specific professional or minority constituencies within CPC-defined parameters. This is distinct from Russia's managed democracy approach — Russia technically permits opposition parties to compete and sometimes win seats, then suppresses them through administrative tools; China simply prohibits genuine competition at the constitutional level. CPC membership is itself a prerequisite for advancement in government, the military, and many major enterprises, making party affiliation a mechanism of social control as well as political organization.
sections.adi_prompt: If China allows eight minor parties to exist, why is it still called a one-party state?

sections.label: Civil Society
sections.title: State-Controlled Socialization and the Great Firewall
sections.blurb: Civil society in China is tightly constrained: NGOs must register with the state, foreign-funded organizations face particular restrictions, and media organizations operate under direct party editorial supervision. The Great Firewall blocks foreign social media platforms, politically sensitive foreign news, and content the party deems destabilizing, preventing citizens from using international platforms for political organizing. State-directed socialization — through CPC-controlled school curricula, state media, and party propaganda — aims to instill conforming political values across the population more systematically than in democratic regimes. Ethnic minority communities, particularly Uighurs in Xinjiang and Tibetans in Tibet, face additional surveillance, restrictions on religious practice, and policies that critics have characterized as cultural assimilation. These cleavages between the Han majority and recognized minority groups generate separatist pressures that the regime manages through centralized repression rather than devolution.
sections.adi_prompt: What is the Great Firewall, and why is it a political tool rather than just a content-filtering system?

sections.label: Political Economy
sections.title: SEZs, Least Private Resource Control, and Growth Legitimacy
sections.blurb: China pursues a distinctive dual economic model: special economic zones (SEZs) along the coast operate under market-oriented rules that attract foreign direct investment, while the interior economy retains state direction and the CPC controls natural resources, making China the course country with the least private resource control. SEZs drove rapid coastal industrialization and a massive rural-to-urban and west-to-east internal migration wave that created both economic growth and regional inequality. Economic growth has served as a primary source of CPC legitimacy — the party claims a mandate based on delivering rising living standards rather than democratic consent. China enacted a one-child policy as a demographic intervention to manage population growth, with significant effects on age structure. Environmental degradation from rapid industrialization has required policy responses including factory relocations, green technology subsidies, and pollution regulation, as the costs of growth have become politically salient.
sections.adi_prompt: How do special economic zones allow China to liberalize its economy selectively without loosening political control?

adi_prompts.quiz: Quiz me on China: ask me about the difference between the NPC and Politburo Standing Committee, what rule by law means, how the Great Firewall works politically, and how SEZs illustrate selective economic liberalization.
adi_prompts.explain: Explain how China's one-party system operates — how does the CPC maintain control over the state, economy, and society while holding nominal elections and allowing eight minor parties to exist?

items.title: Islamic Republic of Iran
items.byline: Dual authority: an unelected Supreme Leader dominates elected institutions through Guardian Council vetting and velayat-e faqih.
items.key_takeaway: Iran is the course's theocratic authoritarian case: velayat-e faqih places a Supreme Leader above all elected officials, and the Guardian Council filters elections to ensure only approved candidates compete.
items.summary: The Islamic Republic of Iran was established following the 1979 revolution that overthrew Shah Pahlavi and replaced a monarchy with a theocracy based on the Shia Islamic doctrine of velayat-e faqih (rule of the Islamic jurist). The Supreme Leader — a senior Shia cleric appointed for life — commands the armed forces, appoints the head of the judiciary, selects half the Guardian Council, and sets overall state policy. Below the Supreme Leader, a popularly elected president oversees the civil service and conducts foreign policy, creating a dual executive in which unelected clerical power dominates elected positions. Iran's legislature, the Majles, is unicameral and elected by direct popular vote, but the Guardian Council vets all candidates for compliance with Islamic values, ensuring only approved figures appear on ballots — making Iranian elections non-competitive in a meaningful democratic sense. Iran is a rentier state deeply dependent on oil revenues, subject to external economic sanctions that constrain its economy. The 2009 Green Movement — mass protests following a disputed presidential election — illustrates how legitimacy crises emerge even in theocratic authoritarian states, and how such regimes suppress oppositional participation. Iran's regime is unique among course countries in grounding state authority explicitly in religious ideology rather than party monopoly or electoral manipulation.
required_cases: Velayat-e faqih doctrine as basis for Supreme Leader's authority
1979 Iranian Revolution: regime change from monarchy to theocracy
Guardian Council vetting candidates for Majles and presidency
2009 Green Movement: mass protest over disputed election results
Runoff election rule: absolute majority required for president
Expediency Council resolving disputes between Majles and Guardian Council
Assembly of Experts: body that selects and can remove Supreme Leader
Reserved seats in Majles for non-Muslim minorities
Rentier state: oil-revenue dependence and external sanctions
Brain drain: emigration of skilled citizens
comparative_themes: rule by law and Sharia-based judiciary
civil society restriction and media censorship

paired_with.title: Federal Republic of Nigeria
paired_with.relation: Rentier state and resource curse comparison; both oil-dependent; both have significant religious cleavages (Shia Islam vs. Muslim/Christian north-south divide)

paired_with.title: Russian Federation
paired_with.relation: Hybrid/authoritarian comparison; both rentier states under external sanctions; both use presidential runoff elections; both suppress civil society

paired_with.title: People's Republic of China
paired_with.relation: Unicameral legislature comparison (Majles vs. NPC); both have legislative bodies constrained by unelected oversight (Guardian Council vs. Politburo Standing Committee)

items.exam_appearance: Used as the primary example of a theocracy, Guardian Council candidate vetting, and the velayat-e faqih doctrine. Appears in MCQs on unicameral legislatures, dual executives, rentier states, and the 2009 Green Movement. Frequently paired with Russia for runoff election comparisons and with China for authoritarian legislature constraints.

sections.label: Government Structure
sections.title: Theocratic Dual Authority: Supreme Leader Above All
sections.blurb: Iran's government is structured around a formal hierarchy in which unelected clerical authority sits above all elected positions. The Supreme Leader — appointed for life by the Assembly of Experts — commands the military, appoints the judiciary head, selects half the Guardian Council, controls state broadcasting, and sets the general direction of state policy. The elected president oversees the civil service and conducts day-to-day foreign policy but operates within boundaries the Supreme Leader defines. This creates a dual executive unique among course countries: while Russia's dual executive pairs president and prime minister in a semi-presidential arrangement, Iran's pairs an unelected religious authority with an elected civilian executive. The Assembly of Experts is itself a body of Islamic scholars elected by citizens, but candidates must be vetted by the Guardian Council — creating a self-reinforcing loop of clerical oversight.
sections.adi_prompt: How does Iran's Supreme Leader maintain authority over the elected president, and why does this make Iran a theocracy rather than just a religious state?

sections.label: Electoral System
sections.title: Direct Elections Under Guardian Council Vetting
sections.blurb: Iran holds direct popular elections for the Majles and the presidency, which superficially resembles a democratic system. However, the Guardian Council — 12 members, six appointed by the Supreme Leader and six judicial nominees confirmed by the Majles — vets all candidates before elections to ensure compliance with Islamic values and loyalty to the regime. Reformists and critics of the system are routinely disqualified, making elections a choice among pre-approved options rather than a genuine contest. Presidential candidates must win an absolute majority in a first or second runoff round. Majles members are elected in single-member and multimember districts; a small number of the 290 seats are reserved for non-Muslim minorities (Christians, Jews, Zoroastrians). Iran formally lacks political party structures — candidates affiliate with loosely organized political factions rather than formal parties, which is a CED-specified distinction.
sections.adi_prompt: How does the Guardian Council's vetting of candidates make Iranian elections fundamentally different from elections in a competitive democracy, even though votes are cast freely?

sections.label: Party System
sections.title: No Formal Parties: Factional Politics Within Clerical Bounds
sections.blurb: Iran lacks formal political party structures — the CED explicitly states this. Political actors organize as loosely formed alliances or factions: reformists who seek moderate policy change within the system, pragmatic conservatives, and hardline conservatives all compete within the narrow space the Guardian Council permits. Because candidates cannot formally organize as parties with platforms, platforms, and open voter linkages, constituency accountability is weaker than in party-based systems. This factional arrangement also means that winning coalitions shift across election cycles as the Guardian Council's vetting decisions determine who is on the ballot. The absence of formal parties makes comparison with other course countries on party system type complex — Iran does not fit the categories of one-party state, dominant-party system, or multiparty system, but rather a vetted-elite system operating under clerical supervision.
sections.adi_prompt: Why does Iran's government say there are no formal political parties, and how do political factions work differently from parties in other course countries?

sections.label: Civil Society
sections.title: Media Censorship, License Revocation, and the Green Movement
sections.blurb: Civil society in Iran operates under severe constraints. The judiciary can suspend or revoke the licenses of publications and media organizations found guilty of publishing anti-religious content or material deemed contrary to national interests. Social media access is restricted and filtered. NGOs face registration requirements and surveillance. The 2009 Green Movement — mass protests following the disputed re-election of President Ahmadinejad — represents the most significant recent example of oppositional political participation in Iran: millions took to the streets challenging electoral integrity, and the state responded with arrests, violence, and escalated restrictions. This event illustrates how even theocratic authoritarian states face legitimacy challenges when citizens question electoral integrity, and how authoritarian regimes suppress such participation more decisively than democratic ones. Ethnic and religious cleavages — between the Shia majority and Sunni minority, and between ethnic Persians and minorities including Kurds and Azerbaijanis — add further dimensions of social tension.
sections.adi_prompt: What was the Green Movement, and what does it reveal about the limits of authoritarian control over citizen participation?

sections.label: Political Economy
sections.title: Rentier State, Brain Drain, and Sanctions
sections.blurb: Iran is a rentier state whose government derives a large share of revenue from oil and gas exports rather than broad-based taxation, reducing citizen leverage over state policy and weakening democratic accountability — a textbook resource curse outcome. External economic sanctions imposed by foreign governments in response to Iran's nuclear program and human rights record constrain the economy, contributing to revenue volatility and currency overvaluation. These conditions have accelerated brain drain: highly educated and skilled Iranians emigrate to escape perceived limitations, corruption, and repression, weakening Iran's long-term human capital. Social policies include gender equity rules in voting and Majles representation and disputes over female access to certain degree programs and sporting events — illustrating how authoritarian regimes adapt social policy to manage legitimacy while maintaining clerical control. Iran's economic model — state control of major industries, reliance on oil revenues, and resistance to IMF-style structural adjustment — places it alongside Russia and Nigeria in the rentier state category.
sections.adi_prompt: How does Iran's dependence on oil revenues reduce government accountability to its citizens, and how do external sanctions make this worse?

adi_prompts.quiz: Quiz me on Iran: ask me about velayat-e faqih, what the Guardian Council does, how the 2009 Green Movement challenged legitimacy, and how Iran's theocratic system compares to China's one-party system.
adi_prompts.explain: Explain Iran's dual authority structure — how does the Supreme Leader's unelected power over elected institutions define Iran as a theocratic authoritarian state rather than a democracy that happens to have religious values?

items.title: United Mexican States
items.byline: From PRI one-party dominance to genuine multiparty competition: federalism, gender quotas, and INE independence define the transition.
items.key_takeaway: Mexico's democratic transition — abolishing el dedazo, creating the INE, and dismantling PRI corporatism — is the course's primary case study in how institutional reforms can transform a dominant-party authoritarian system into genuine multiparty competition.
items.summary: Mexico is a federal presidential republic that underwent a landmark democratic transition across the 1990s and early 2000s, shifting from seven decades of Institutional Revolutionary Party (PRI) one-party dominance to genuine multiparty competition. The transition involved specific institutional reforms: abolishing el dedazo (the PRI president's informal power to hand-pick a successor), creating the independent National Electoral Institute (INE, formerly IFE) to administer elections free from government control, privatizing state-owned corporations to reduce the PRI's patronage base, and decentralizing power to subnational governments. The 2000 election of Vicente Fox (PAN), the first non-PRI president in 71 years, marks the pivotal democratization moment. Mexico's mixed electoral system — combining single-member districts and proportional representation — and gender quotas for party lists have increased representation compared to purely FPTP systems. Mexico is a federal state with 31 states plus a federal entity, each with their own constitutions and governors, contrasting with China's unitary centralism. Key social movements including the 1994 Zapatista uprising in Chiapas — protesting NAFTA and indigenous marginalization — illustrate internal actors challenging state authority from below. As a party to NAFTA (now USMCA), Mexico's economy was significantly reshaped by trade liberalization, generating maquiladora zones, internal migration from south to north, and a growing middle class alongside persistent inequality.
required_cases: PRI one-party dominance (1929–2000) and corporatist system
El dedazo: PRI president's informal successor-selection practice
Vicente Fox (PAN) 2000 election: first non-PRI president
INE/IFE creation as independent electoral management body
Mixed electoral system: SMD + PR with gender quotas
Zapatista uprising in Chiapas (1994): indigenous autonomy demand
NAFTA/USMCA effects: maquiladoras, internal migration, north-south disparity
Pemex partial privatization and energy sector reform
Presidential plurality rule (most votes wins, no runoff required)
Corporatist to pluralist interest group transition

paired_with.title: Federal Republic of Nigeria
paired_with.relation: Democratic transition comparison: both transitioned from authoritarian rule to presidential multiparty systems; both created independent electoral commissions

paired_with.title: Russian Federation
paired_with.relation: Democratic transition contrast: both had dominant-party authoritarian systems; Mexico genuinely democratized while Russia reversed under Putin

paired_with.title: United Kingdom
paired_with.relation: Electoral system comparison: Mexico's mixed SMD+PR system vs. UK's pure FPTP, and their contrasting effects on party system fragmentation

items.exam_appearance: Primary case for democratic transition, corporatism-to-pluralism shift, and INE electoral reform. Appears in MCQs on mixed electoral systems, gender quotas, the Zapatista movement as an autonomy demand, NAFTA effects, and maquiladora zones. Frequently paired with Russia as a divergent transition case and with Nigeria as a parallel presidential-system democratization.

sections.label: Government Structure
sections.title: Federal Presidential System with Strong Executive
sections.blurb: Mexico is a presidential federal republic in which the president serves as both head of state and head of government, commander in chief, and leader of the civil service. The president is limited to a single six-year term (sexenio) with no re-election, a rule designed to prevent the concentration of power that characterized the PRI era. The bicameral Congress consists of the Chamber of Deputies (500 members: 300 elected in SMDs, 200 by PR party lists) and the Senate (128 members: 96 in three-seat state constituencies, 32 by PR). The Supreme Court has the power of judicial review, and subsequent constitutional reforms have aimed to make it more independent. Mexico's 31 states each have their own constitutions, governors, and legislatures, making federalism a real structural feature rather than nominal — contrasting with China's unitary centralism in which provincial leaders implement central directives.
sections.adi_prompt: How does Mexico's single-term presidential limit prevent the kind of power concentration that the PRI maintained for 71 years?

sections.label: Electoral System
sections.title: Mixed SMD + PR with Gender Quotas and INE Oversight
sections.blurb: Mexico's mixed electoral system combines 300 single-member districts (plurality rule) with 200 party-list seats allocated by proportional representation for the Chamber of Deputies, and a three-seat senatorial constituency model for the Senate. Gender quotas are applied to the party list system, requiring parties to field a minimum share of female candidates — this has measurably increased women's representation in the legislature compared to purely FPTP systems. Presidential elections use a simple national plurality: the candidate with the most votes wins outright, with no runoff required (unlike Iran and Russia). The National Electoral Institute (INE, formerly IFE) is an independent body created specifically to administer elections free from government manipulation, a key institutional reform of the democratic transition. The INE's independence directly contrasts with Iran's Guardian Council, which restricts ballots, and Russia's Central Election Commission, which operates under presidential influence.
sections.adi_prompt: How do gender quotas in Mexico's party lists work, and why does a mixed SMD + PR system produce different representation outcomes than the UK's pure FPTP system?

sections.label: Party System
sections.title: From PRI Corporatism to Genuine Multiparty Competition
sections.blurb: For most of the 20th century, the PRI maintained one-party dominance through a corporatist system: it organized labor unions, peasant associations, and business groups as officially recognized single peak associations (SPAs) that channeled their interests through the party rather than competing independently. El dedazo — the sitting president's informal power to hand-pick the next presidential candidate — epitomized the system's anti-democratic character. Democratic transition dismantled these mechanisms: the INE was created to administer elections independently, el dedazo was abolished, state-owned enterprises were privatized to reduce PRI patronage resources, and power was decentralized to subnational governments. Today, Mexico has a genuine multiparty system in which PAN (center-right), PRD (center-left), and PRI compete, alongside newer parties. This shift from corporatism to pluralism is the CED's named example of how interest group systems can change over time.
sections.adi_prompt: What was el dedazo, and why was abolishing it essential to Mexico's democratic transition?

sections.label: Civil Society
sections.title: Social Movements, Indigenous Rights, and the Zapatistas
sections.blurb: Mexico's civil society has grown more robust with democratization, but significant challenges remain, including drug trafficking organization violence that threatens state authority in some regions. The 1994 Zapatista uprising in Chiapas — led by the EZLN and its spokesperson Subcomandante Marcos — is the CED's named case of a social movement demanding autonomy (not secession) for indigenous communities in response to NAFTA's expected effects on subsistence farming. The Zapatistas illustrate how grassroots movements can apply pressure on the state from below and how economic liberalization can generate political backlash among marginalized populations. Mexico's ethnic cleavage — between the indigenous/Amerindian population and mestizos and whites — is a significant social division, with regional overlap between the indigenous-majority south and the wealthier, more industrialized north. Over time, civil society has shifted from corporatist state channels toward more pluralist, autonomous advocacy organizations.
sections.adi_prompt: What did the Zapatista uprising in 1994 have to do with NAFTA, and why is it classified as a demand for autonomy rather than independence?

sections.label: Political Economy
sections.title: NAFTA, Maquiladoras, Internal Migration, and Mixed Liberalization Outcomes
sections.blurb: Mexico's economy was substantially reshaped by NAFTA (1994, later replaced by USMCA), which eliminated most tariffs with Canada and the US and drove the growth of maquiladora assembly zones along the northern border. These zones attracted foreign direct investment and manufacturing employment, contributing to north-south regional inequality as the northern states industrialized faster than the agrarian south. NAFTA also removed agricultural subsidies that supported subsistence farmers, accelerating rural-to-urban and south-to-north internal migration. The overall effect was mixed: Mexico's middle class grew and national income rose, but inequality persisted and marginalized rural communities experienced displacement. Pemex — Mexico's state oil company — underwent partial privatization as part of economic liberalization, contrasting with Putin's re-nationalization of Russian oil. Mexico's experience is the CED's primary example of ISI being replaced by trade liberalization, with mixed social consequences that generated both growth and political backlash (Zapatistas).
sections.adi_prompt: How did NAFTA's removal of agricultural subsidies lead to the Zapatista uprising, and what does this tell us about trade liberalization's effects on different social groups?

adi_prompts.quiz: Quiz me on Mexico: ask me about el dedazo, the INE's role in democratic transition, how the Zapatista movement connects to NAFTA, and how Mexico's democratic transition compares to Russia's reversal.
adi_prompts.explain: Explain Mexico's democratic transition — what specific institutional changes turned the PRI's one-party system into genuine multiparty competition, and why didn't Russia make the same transition?

items.title: Federal Republic of Nigeria
items.byline: Africa's most populous democracy: 250+ ethnic groups, oil-dependent rentier state, and persistent governance challenges since 1999.
items.key_takeaway: Nigeria is the course's primary case for overlapping ethnic, religious, and regional cleavages; the 25% geographic presidential threshold; and the tensions between oil-revenue rentier dynamics and democratic accountability.
items.summary: Nigeria returned to civilian rule in 1999 after decades of alternating military governments, establishing a federal presidential republic modeled broadly on a separation of powers with a bicameral National Assembly. As the most populous country in Africa with more than 250 ethnic groups — including the three dominant groups of Hausa-Fulani (north), Yoruba (southwest), and Igbo (southeast) — Nigeria's political system is deeply shaped by ethnic and religious cleavages. The north is predominantly Muslim while the south is predominantly Christian and animist, creating a north-south religious divide that overlaps with regional economic inequality. To manage these cleavages, the presidential election requires not just the most votes but also at least 25 percent of the vote in two-thirds of Nigeria's states — a geographic distribution requirement that forces candidates to build broad national coalitions. Nigeria is a rentier state: the Nigerian National Petroleum Corporation (NNPC) operates in joint ventures with foreign multinationals, and oil revenues dominate the federal budget, generating resource curse dynamics including corruption and weak accountability. The People's Democratic Party (PDP) controlled the presidency from 1999 to 2015, when the All Progressives Congress (APC) defeated it — a landmark democratic transfer of power. Social movements including MOSOP (led by Ken Saro-Wiwa until his execution in 1995) and MEND have challenged state authority over Niger Delta oil revenues, illustrating internal actors pressing for resource distribution equity.
required_cases: 1999 transition to civilian rule under Obasanjo
25% geographic threshold rule for presidential elections
INEC as independent electoral management body
PDP-to-APC power transfer in 2015 as democratic consolidation milestone
NNPC joint ventures with multinational oil companies
MOSOP and Ken Saro-Wiwa: civil society challenging oil extraction
MEND: militant movement demanding Niger Delta revenue redistribution
Boko Haram: Islamist insurgency from north-south religious cleavage
Ethnic quotas in party structures and Federal Character Commission
Sharia courts in northern states under federalism
Brain drain: emigration of skilled Nigerians
comparative_themes: ethnic quotas and federal character principle

paired_with.title: United Mexican States
paired_with.relation: Democratic transition comparison: both moved from authoritarian rule to presidential multiparty systems; both have independent electoral commissions; Nigeria's geographic threshold vs. Mexico's plurality rule is a classic exam pairing

paired_with.title: Islamic Republic of Iran
paired_with.relation: Rentier state and resource curse comparison; both oil-dependent; both have significant religious cleavages and corruption challenges

paired_with.title: Russian Federation
paired_with.relation: Rentier state comparison: both oil-dependent; contrast in how resources shape political accountability (democratic vs. managed-democracy context)

items.exam_appearance: Primary case for the 25% geographic threshold, MOSOP as civil society, and overlapping ethnic-religious-regional cleavages. Used in MCQs comparing presidential election rules (with Mexico and Iran), independent electoral commissions (with Mexico), and rentier state dynamics (with Iran and Russia). Frequently appears in data-analysis questions on HDI, Gini, and oil-revenue tables.

sections.label: Government Structure
sections.title: Federal Presidential System with Impeachment Powers
sections.blurb: Nigeria is a federal presidential republic in which the president serves as head of state, head of government, commander in chief, and head of the civil service. The president and vice president are separately elected on a joint ticket. The bicameral National Assembly consists of the Senate (108 members: three per state, reflecting federal equality) and the House of Representatives; both chambers must approve legislation, and the National Assembly can impeach the president by a two-thirds supermajority for gross misconduct — a higher threshold than in many presidential systems. Under Nigeria's federalism, the 36 states and the Federal Capital Territory have their own constitutions, governors, and assemblies. Federalism also allows northern states to operate Sharia courts for personal law matters, illustrating how a federal structure permits subnational legal variation that a unitary system like Iran's could not accommodate.
sections.adi_prompt: How does Nigeria's federal structure allow northern states to have Sharia courts while maintaining a secular national constitution?

sections.label: Electoral System
sections.title: 25% Geographic Threshold and INEC Independence
sections.blurb: Nigeria's presidential election system is unique among course countries: to win outright, a candidate must receive the most votes nationally AND secure at least 25 percent of the vote in two-thirds (24 of 36) of Nigeria's states. This geographic distribution requirement reflects the country's federal character and forces candidates to build ethnically and regionally diverse coalitions rather than relying solely on the base vote of one region or ethnic group. If no candidate meets both requirements, a runoff follows. Legislative elections use simple SMD plurality rules. The Independent National Electoral Commission (INEC) was created to administer elections independently, paralleling Mexico's INE as an institutional reform to reduce fraud and build confidence — both countries created independent EMBs as part of democratization from authoritarian pasts. INEC's effectiveness has improved over time but credibility challenges persist, reflecting Nigeria's ongoing democratic consolidation.
sections.adi_prompt: Why does Nigeria require presidential candidates to win 25 percent of the vote in two-thirds of states, and what problem was this rule designed to solve?

sections.label: Party System
sections.title: Two-Party Dominance, Ethnic Quotas, and the 2015 Transition
sections.blurb: Nigeria's party system features two dominant parties — the People's Democratic Party (PDP) and the All Progressives Congress (APC) — that have alternated control of the presidency, plus a number of smaller parties. The PDP dominated from 1999 to 2015; the APC's defeat of the PDP in the 2015 election (when Buhari defeated incumbent Goodluck Jonathan) was a landmark moment for democratic consolidation, marking the first peaceful, opposition-led transfer of presidential power in Nigeria's history. Nigeria's 30-plus registered parties must comply with the Federal Character principle — ensuring geographic and ethnic diversity in party leadership and government appointments. Ethnic quotas in party structures reflect the political salience of Nigeria's 250+ ethnic groups. Unlike Mexico's transition, which involved breaking one dominant party's hold on power through institutional reform, Nigeria's consolidation has involved genuine electoral competition between two broad coalition parties from the start of the Fourth Republic.
sections.adi_prompt: Why was the 2015 APC victory over the PDP considered a major milestone for Nigerian democracy, and how does this compare to Mexico's 2000 PAN victory?

sections.label: Civil Society
sections.title: MOSOP, MEND, Boko Haram, and Overlapping Cleavages
sections.blurb: Nigeria has one of the most complex cleavage structures among course countries: ethnic divisions among more than 250 groups (with the Hausa-Fulani, Yoruba, and Igbo as the three most politically salient), a north-south religious divide between Muslim-majority and Christian/animist-majority regions, and regional economic inequality between the oil-producing Niger Delta south and other areas. The Movement for the Survival of the Ogoni People (MOSOP), led by Ken Saro-Wiwa until his execution by the Abacha military government in 1995, is the CED's named civil society example for Nigeria — an advocacy organization challenging Shell Oil's extraction practices in Ogoni lands. The Movement for the Emancipation of the Niger Delta (MEND) is a militant variant demanding revenue redistribution through attacks on oil infrastructure. Boko Haram — an Islamist insurgency in northeastern Nigeria — illustrates how unmanaged ethnic and religious cleavages can escalate into terrorism that threatens regime stability and state authority.
sections.adi_prompt: How do MOSOP and MEND differ from each other in their approach, and what do both reveal about Nigeria's resource curse?

sections.label: Political Economy
sections.title: NNPC, Rentier Dynamics, and the Resource Curse
sections.blurb: Nigeria is a rentier state in which oil and gas revenues dominate the federal budget. The Nigerian National Petroleum Corporation (NNPC) operates in joint ventures with foreign multinational companies to extract and sell oil from the Niger Delta, making Nigeria's resource sector dependent on foreign investment while the state maintains ownership. Classic resource curse outcomes are visible: heavy dependence on a single export sector, lack of economic diversification, revenue volatility tied to global oil prices, elevated corruption, and weak accountability to citizens who are not taxed. Nigeria joined ECOWAS (Economic Community of West African States), a supranational regional organization with sovereign powers over trade policy. Brain drain — emigration of educated Nigerians seeking better opportunities abroad — weakens long-term human capital. Gender education gaps between Nigeria's more educated south and the less-educated, more conservative north illustrate how geographic and religious cleavages translate into social policy inequality within a single federal state.
sections.adi_prompt: How does NNPC's joint-venture model with foreign oil companies reflect Nigeria's rentier state dynamics, and why does this reduce government accountability to ordinary citizens?

adi_prompts.quiz: Quiz me on Nigeria: ask me about the 25% geographic threshold, what MOSOP and MEND are, how Nigeria's ethnic and religious cleavages interact, and how Nigeria's presidential election rules compare to Mexico's.
adi_prompts.explain: Explain Nigeria's resource curse — how does dependence on NNPC oil revenues affect government accountability, corruption, and the country's ability to diversify its economy?
